slides.title: The Identity Function 🪞
slides.content:
Imagine we want a simple utility function `mirror()` that takes in an argument and returns that exact same argument back unchanged.

How do we type this function if we want it to accept numbers, strings, booleans, and objects?

slides.title: Using 'any'
slides.content: The naive approach is to use the `any` type.

quizData.question: If you use `function mirror(arg: any): any`, what is the final type of the `output` variable above?

options.text: It accurately infer it as a 'string'.
It becomes 'any', completely destroying all TypeScript safety going forward.

quizData.explanation: Because the function definitively returns `any`, everything that comes out of it permanently loses its type formatting. We passed a string in, but TS forgot it was a string on the way out!

slides.title: Losing Information 🕳️
slides.content:
Using `any` here is terrible because we LOSE the information about what type we originally passed in.

We need a way to say: "I will accept an argument of ANY type, but the function's return type MUST EXACTLY MATCH whatever type that happens to be!"

slides.title: The Solution 🚀
slides.content: To solve this massive architectural issue, advanced languages introduce a concept known as **Generics**.
